question: how to raised vegetable garden
title: How to Start a Raised Vegetable Garden the Right Way — Soil First, Plants Second
metaDescription: Starting a raised vegetable garden is mostly a soil problem, not a planting problem. Scotty walks through the decay cycle approach to building beds that actually grow food worth eating.
content:
# How to Start a Raised Vegetable Garden the Right Way — Soil First, Plants Second

Hey everybody. I get asked about raised vegetable gardens a lot, and I think most of the advice out there is getting it backwards.

People spend a lot of time asking about which plants to grow, what trellis to use, when to water. All of that matters a little bit. But the real question — the one that determines whether your garden actually produces food worth eating — is what are you growing those plants in?

I run sixteen raised beds in my backyard in Houston, Texas. I've made the mistakes. I've grown in bad soil and watched plants limp along producing mediocre vegetables. And I've grown in rich, living, compost-amended soil and watched plants explode with production. The difference is the soil. Always the soil.

So this is a guide about raised vegetable gardening, but it's really a guide about building the right soil. Get the soil right and the plants mostly take care of themselves.

## Why Raised Beds Work — and When They Don't

Raised beds work because they give you control over your soil environment in a way that in-ground gardening in typical urban or suburban soil doesn't. Most Houston-area soil is heavy clay. It compacts, drains poorly, and when it dries out in the summer heat, it bakes into something close to concrete. Trying to grow vegetables in it without significant amendment is a losing game.

A raised bed lets you start fresh. You build the bed, fill it with the right material, and give your plants a root environment that's actually conducive to growing.

Raised beds also warm up faster in spring. The soil drains better than compacted ground, which means it heats up sooner and plants can go in earlier. In Houston that's not as critical as in northern gardens, but it still matters in February and early March.

The catch — and this is where a lot of raised bed gardeners go wrong — is that a raised bed is only as good as what you fill it with. Fill it with cheap bagged topsoil from a big box store and you've got a raised box of mediocre dirt. The bed structure doesn't automatically give you good growing conditions. The biology in the soil does.

## Building Your Beds: Size, Depth, and Material

For most backyard situations, the sweet spot is three feet wide by eight or nine feet long. Three feet means you can reach the center from either side without stepping in the bed. That matters a lot — stepping in the bed compacts the soil and breaks up the fungal networks you're trying to build.

Depth should be at least twelve inches, and eighteen is better. Most vegetable root systems want twelve inches of loose, well-draining growing medium. Go shallower and you're forcing roots into whatever is below the bed — which in Houston is usually compacted clay.

For materials, cedar is the traditional choice because it resists rot. Untreated pine works and is cheaper, but it will break down in three to five years in a hot, humid climate. I've had pine beds bow out and fall apart on me. Expect to replace them. Avoid treated lumber unless you're using newer ACQ or CA-B treated wood — older CCA-treated lumber contains arsenic and you don't want that near your vegetables.

No bottom is required. Leaving the bed open to the ground below is better — it lets earthworms move up from the native soil and gives deep-rooted plants somewhere to go.

## What to Fill Your Beds With

Soil in a raised bed needs to be a living medium, not just a growing medium. There's a real difference.

A living medium has active soil biology — bacteria, fungi, nematodes, protozoa — cycling nutrients in real time. A growing medium just holds the plant upright and stores whatever water and fertilizer you add. One produces living food. The other produces plants.

My preferred approach: start with roughly fifty percent good garden soil and fifty percent finished compost. Not bagged compost from a store shelf — finished compost that has been properly heated and cured. Make your own if you can. If you can't, find a local source where you know what went into the pile.

That ratio gets adjusted over time. Every season I top-dress my beds with an inch or two of finished compost. I don't till it in — I let the earthworms and soil biology incorporate it from below. Over two or three seasons, the beds develop a rich, dark, crumbly soil with excellent structure and thriving biology.

Oregon State Extension puts it simply: good compost feeds the biology of your soil, and that biology feeds your plants. That's the whole model. You are not feeding plants directly. You are feeding the microbes that feed the plants. The distinction matters enormously.

## The Decay Cycle

Growth and decay are two sides of the same cycle. Your vegetables grow. They produce food. At the end of the season, the plant material that's left over goes into the compost. The compost breaks down into finished organic material. That finished material goes back into the beds. The beds produce better vegetables next season than they did the season before.

This is what Albert Howard was describing in his work nearly a century ago. The Law of Return. Whatever you take from the soil, you return to the soil. A raised vegetable garden that follows this cycle gets better every year. A raised bed garden that mines the soil without returning organic matter gets worse every year.

In practice: at the end of each growing season, pull your spent plants and put them in the compost pile — don't send them to the landfill, that's the organic matter leaving your system. Top-dress the beds with finished compost before the next season. Let the biology do the incorporation. Repeat.

It takes a little bit of discipline to close that loop consistently. But once you're doing it, the beds start to self-improve in a way that feels almost magical — until you understand the biology, and then it just feels obvious.

## Planting Your First Raised Bed

Once you've got your beds built and filled with good soil, you're ready to plant. A few things that work well in the South Texas climate:

Start with herbs. You're only harvesting leaves from herbs, which means there's no waiting for fruit to develop. Basil, parsley, cilantro, chives, and rosemary are all easy to grow and transform what you eat every day. Fresh herbs from your own garden taste fundamentally different from the dried stuff in a jar.

For vegetables, tomatoes and peppers are the backbone of a Houston summer garden. Get a good trellis in early — tomatoes are vines and they want to climb. Cucumbers are another high-producer that rewards trellis investment. Eggplant does beautifully in the heat. For cooler months — fall through early spring — greens, root vegetables, and brassicas thrive in South Texas.

Give your plants what they need and then leave them alone as much as possible. Living soil wants to do its job. Trust the biology you've built.

## Watering and Maintenance

Good soil structure — the kind you get with compost-rich beds — holds water better than compacted soil or pure topsoil. You'll water a little bit less than you'd expect, especially after the first season when the soil biology is established.

A simple drip irrigation system is worth the investment in a Houston summer. It puts water at the root zone rather than wetting the foliage, which cuts disease pressure. It also keeps the soil consistently moist rather than cycling through wet and dry extremes, which is better for the biology.

Mulch the surface of your beds. A layer of straw or wood chips on the soil surface cuts evaporation, keeps soil temperatures more stable, and feeds the biology as it slowly breaks down. Bare soil in a Houston summer dries out and bakes in a way that stresses the microbial community you've worked to build.

Y'all, a raised vegetable garden is one of the best investments you can make in your family's food quality. But do it right — build the soil first, feed the biology, close the decay cycle loop, and let the living system do the work. That's how you get vegetables worth eating.

---
